learning_outcomes.description: Understand key theories, concepts, and processes involved in additional language learning.
Effectively analyse a range of issues influencing second and foreign language learning, including learner and contextual factors.
Reflect on and critically evaluate your own language learning experiences in light of second language acquisition theories to enhance your ongoing academic development as a language learner.

Apply core learning principles and knowledge of the history and development of second and foreign language learning to plan, justify, and evaluate reflective and evidence-based approaches to language learning.
Demonstrate effective interaction and collaboration skills in diverse communicative and group-work settings to support shared learning outcomes.
Effectively use appropriate digital technologies to translate theoretical concepts into meaningful language learning experiences (e.g. digital artefacts).

assessments.task_description:
You are required to complete a weekly pre-class online quiz before class session. To prepare for the quiz, please complete the assigned readings and listen to the online lecture each week. Quiz questions may include multiple-choice, True/False, matching, and fill-in-the-blank formats. Each quiz consists of 10 questions, and the quiz link will be available on Blackboard/ Assessment/ Assessment Task 1: Pre-class Quiz.

Each quiz will open at 12pm on Tuesday and will be due at 2pm the following Monday, giving you six days to prepare and complete the quiz at a convenient time. For example, Quiz 2 opens at 12pm on Tuesday, 28 July 2026, and is due at 2pm on Monday, 03 August 2026. When a Monday falls on a public holiday, the quiz will be due on the Tuesday of that week at 2pm instead. Please check the due date for each quiz below.

You have only ONE attempt to complete the quiz. You will have a maximum of 15 minutes to complete it. An automatic timer will begin when you start the quiz, so please complete the required reading BEFORE starting the quiz.

HURDLE REQUIREMENT

There are 10 quizzes scheduled across the semester in Weeks 2-4 and 6-12. You must complete at least 6 out of the 10 quizzes to meet the hurdle requirement. Failure to meet this requirement will result in failing the course.

Generative AI & MT Statement

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools and/or Machine Translation (MT) technologies to support their preparation, such use must be appropriately acknowledged or referenced in line with UQ’s academic integrity expectations. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
This is an in-class exam held during the Week 5 Tutorial

The exam will be done on paper.

Duration: 90 minutes (plus 10 minutes preparation time).

In this assessment, you will analyse second language learner data using the course content from Modules 1-4, focusing mainly on the Contrastive Analysis Hypothesis (CAH) and Error Analysis (EA). You will be provided with two short written texts produced by English language learners from different first language backgrounds, reflecting on their language learning experiences. The texts contain a range of linguistic errors (e.g. grammar and vocabulary) commonly found in second language learning.

You are required to identify and analyse selected errors in the learner texts and explain the potential reasons for these errors drawing on CAH and EA (e.g. transfer errors or developmental errors). You will also be asked to propose practising activities that address selected learner errors, demonstrating how theoretical insights can inform informal learning practice. In addition, you will reflect on similarities between the observed learner errors and your own experiences of learning a second language (e.g. Korean, Japanese, Spanish...) and evaluate the usefulness of CAH and EA for language learning in general.

This assessment evaluates your ability to apply SLA theory to authentic learner data and language learning situations.

Generative AI & MT Statement

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This is an in-person, paper-based assessment task.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.category: Creative Production/ Exhibition, Product/ Design, Project
assessments.task_description:
Form a group of three and design a website to introduce SLA Theories using lay language and personal experience to non-SLA audiences including key aspects of the theories, personal stories, learning implications and review of SLA research. The length of the website is approximately 1500 words (i.e. 500 words per student). Procedures, guidelines and format will be discussed at length in the tutorials.

Group registration will take place in class in Week 6.

Generative AI & MT Statement

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools and/or Machine Translation (MT) technologies to support their preparation, such use must be appropriately acknowledged or referenced in line with UQ’s academic integrity expectations. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
You are required to:

(1) watch a series of video demonstrations of EFL/ESL language classroom learning and discuss these methods during the tutorials;

(2) participate in two language lessons in which basic phrases and vocabulary of a less commonly taught language (e.g. Vietnamese) are taught and practised. These language lessons will be taught within the Week 11 and Week 12 tutorials; and

(3) complete a language quiz after attending the two language sessions to assess how much you have learned. Completing the language quiz will be part of the assessment, but the actual score you receive will not be taken into account.

Based on the above participation, you are then required to write a reflective essay on your language learning experience. You are required to:

  • Reflect on the language learning experience in the two language classes, in comparison with the three videos discussed during the tutorials, as well as aspects you found more and less challenging for your learning.
  • Relate your experience to the language learning theories and approaches discussed in the textbook and throughout the course.
  • Consider a range of factors, including the effectiveness of using only the target language, the role of written language in spoken language learning, and possible differences between domains such as pronunciation, vocabulary, grammar, and so on.

Procedures, guidelines and essay format will be presented in the tutorials.

Word length - 2,000 words

Generative AI & MT Statement

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools and/or Machine Translation (MT) technologies to support their preparation, such use must be appropriately acknowledged or referenced in line with UQ’s academic integrity expectations. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
